title: HYRISK tutorial: failure assessment of a coastal dyke
text: case study focused stability analysis dyke described (Ferson Tucker 2006). dyke revetments made masonry blocks subject wave action depicted schematically Figure . stability estimated difference dike strength minus stress acting \(Z = strength - stress =\Delta.D -\frac{H.\tan(\alpha)}{\cos(\alpha).M.s^{0.5}}\) \(\Delta\) relative density revetment blocks, \(D\) thickness, \(\alpha\) slope revetment. wave characteristics significant wave height \(H\), offshore peak wave steepness \(s\). factor \(M\) reflects risk analyst\(^{'}\)s vision uncertainty related model , .e. ability reproduce reality. \(Z\ge0\), dike stable (strength greater stress); unstable otherwise. study focused estimate probability \(Z\) become negative, considered measure dike reliability. assessment function coded follows:
code: FUN = function(X){   delta = X[1]   D = X[2]   alpha = X[3]   M = X[4]   H = X[5]   s = X[6] return(delta*D-(H*tan(alpha)/(cos(alpha)*M*sqrt(s)))) }

title: HYRISK tutorial: failure assessment of a coastal dyke
text: first step focuses uncertainty representation. aims selecting appropriate mathematical tool represent uncertainty considered parameter. available options : interval, possibility distribution (trapezoidal triangular, see e.g. (Baudrit Dubois 2006)), probability distribution (normal, lognormal, beta, triangle, uniform, Gumbel user-defined), probability distribution imprecise parameters, .e. family parametric probability distributions represented p-box (Ferson et al. 2002). sake clarity, use generic term imprecise probability designate uncertainty representation tool. procedure hyriskR first uses create_input function define input variables (imprecise, random fixed); instance setting values bounds interval, mean standard deviation normal probability distribution, etc. Second, create_distr function assigns corresponding distribution (probability possibility) uncertain input. visualisation function plot_input also implemented handle different representation forms.
code: #INPUT PARAMETERS ninput = 8  #Number of input parameters  # input parameters for the model  # + subvariables in case of a 2-level propagation input = vector(mode="list", length =ninput)  input[[1]]=create_input(                         name= expression(Delta),                         type="possi", distr="interval",                          param=c(1.6,1.65)) input[[2]]=create_input(                         name="D",                         type="possi", distr="interval",                          param=c(0.68,0.72)) input[[3]]=create_input(                         name=expression(alpha),                         type="possi", distr="triangle",                         param=c(0.309,0.318,0.328)) input[[4]]=create_input(                         name="M",                         type="possi", distr="trapeze",                         param=c(3,4,5,5.2)) input[[5]]=create_input(                         name="H",                         type="impr proba",distr="user",                         param=c(7,8),                         quser=qweibull,ruser=rweibull) input[[6]]=create_input(                         name="s",                         type="proba",distr="normal",                         param=c(0.03,0.006)) input[[7]]=create_input(                         name="k",                         type="possi",distr="interval",                         param=c(10,12)) input[[8]]=create_input(                         name=expression(lambda),                         type="possi",distr="interval",                         param=c(1.2,1.5))  #CREATION OF THE DISTRIBUTIONS ASSOCIATED TO THE PARAMETERS input=create_distr(input) #VISUALISATION of INPUT UNCERTAINTY REPRESENTATION plot_input(input)

title: HYRISK tutorial: failure assessment of a coastal dyke
text: output propagation procedure takes form N random intervals form \([\underline{Z}_k,\overline{Z}_k]\), \(k=1,...,N\). information can summarized form pair upper lower cumulative probability distributions (CDFs), form p-box closely related upper lower probabilities Dempster (Dempster 1967), belief functions Shafer (Shafer et al. 1976) proposed (Baudrit, Couso, Dubois 2007). following code provides output propagation phase using plot_cdf function. situations, analysts may comfortable deriving unique probability distribution. order support decision-making less extreme indicator using either probability bounds, (Dubois Guyonnet 2011) proposed weight bounds index \(w\), reflects attitude decision-maker risk (.e. degree risk aversion) resulting distribution \(F\) holds \(F^{-1}(x) = w.\overline{F}^{-1}(x)+(1-w).\underline{F}^{-1}(x)\) \(x\) quantile level ranging 0 1. can done using summmary_1cdf function follows using two aversion weight values respectively 30 50\(\%\).  functionalities hyriskR enable summarise p-box depending statistical quantity interest supporting decision making process. interest quantile given level (say 75\(\%\)), function provides corresponding interval. interest global measure epistemic uncertainty affecting whole probability distribution \(Z\), uncertainty function computes area within lower upper probability distribution. interest probability Z decision threshold zero, PROBA_INTERVAL function provides corresponding upper lower bound probability.
code: #Visualisation plot_cdf(Z0_IRS, xlab="Z", ylab="Cumulative Probability", main="", lwd=3.5) grid(lwd=1.5) #Summary with one CDF Z50 = summary_1cdf(Z0_IRS, aversion=0.5)##risk aversion of 50% lines(ecdf(Z50), col=4, lwd=3.5) Z30 = summary_1cdf(Z0_IRS, aversion=0.3)##risk aversion of 30% lines(ecdf(Z30), col=3, lwd=3.5) legend("topleft",c("upper CDF","lower CDF","Aversion weight 50%", "Aversion weight 30%"), col=c(1,2,4,3), lwd=2.5, bty="n") #Interval of quantiles level=0.75##quantile level quant<-quan_interval(Z0_IRS, level) print(paste("Quantile inf: ", round(quant$Qlow,2))) ## [1] "Quantile inf:  0.33" print(paste("Quantile sup: ", round(quant$Qupp,2))) ## [1] "Quantile sup:  0.79" #Global indicator of uncertainty unc = uncertainty(Z0_IRS) print(paste("Epistemic uncertainty : ", round(unc,2),sep="")) ## [1] "Epistemic uncertainty : 50.96" #Interval of probabilities thres=0.## decision threshold prob = proba_interval(Z0_IRS, thres) print(paste("Probability inf: ",round(prob$Plow,2))) ## [1] "Probability inf:  0" print(paste("Probability sup: ",round(prob$Pupp,2))) ## [1] "Probability sup:  0.07"

title: HYRISK tutorial: failure assessment of a coastal dyke
text: last step focuses sensitivity analysis. approach, based pinching method (Ferson Tucker 2006), implemented using PINCHING_fun sensi_pinching functions. following, analyse sensitivity 8th input parameter.  pinching, impact epistemic reduction can summarised using sensi_pinching function terms : * Reduction area CDFs. * Reduction interval probability exceeding zero threshold. * Reduction interval quantile, instance \(75\%\).
code: ################################################# #### PINCHING - scale ################################################# Z0p = pinching_fun(     which=8,##scale parameter     value=1.4, ##pinched at the scalar value of 1.4     N=1000,     input,     FUN,     choice_opt,     param_opt,     mode="IRS"     ) #VISU - PROPAGATION plot_cdf(Z0_IRS, xlab="Z", ylab="CDF", main="", lwd=3.5) plot_cdf(Z0p, color1="gray75", color2="orange", new=FALSE, lwd=3.5) legend("topleft",c("upper CDF","lower CDF","upper CDF after pinching", "lower CDF after pinching"),col=c(1,2,"gray75","orange"),lwd=2.5,bty="n") # quantile mode sensi.quan = sensi_pinching(Z0_IRS, Z0p, mode="quantile", level=0.75) print(paste("Quantile-based sensitivity measure: ",round(sensi.quan,2),sep="")) ## [1] "Quantile-based sensitivity measure: 26.15" # proba mode sensi.proba = sensi_pinching(Z0_IRS, Z0p, mode="proba", threshold=0) print(paste("Proba-based sensitivity measure: ",round(sensi.proba,2),sep="")) ## [1] "Proba-based sensitivity measure: 56.76" # global mode sensi.global = sensi_pinching(Z0_IRS, Z0p, mode="global", disc=0.01) print(paste("global sensitivity measure: ",round(sensi.global,2),sep="")) ## [1] "global sensitivity measure: 25.72"

text: assessment function coded follows:
code: FUN<-function(X){     UER = X[1]     EF = X[2]     I = X[3]     C = X[4]     ED = X[5]     return(UER*I*C*EF*ED/(70*70*365)) }

title: Possibilities and probabilities with hyriskR
text: first step focuses uncertainty representation. aims selecting appropriate mathematical tool represent uncertainty considered parameter. procedure hyriskR first uses create_input function define input variables (imprecise, random fixed). Second, create_distr function assigns corresponding distribution (probability possibility) uncertain input. case considered, different parameters either represented probabilities (argument type=proba) possibilities, either triangular trapezoidal, (argument type=possi). instance, type=“possi” distr=“triangle”, parameter represented triangular possibility distribution. param argument defines triangle. example c(0,1,2) corresponds possibility distribution core={1} support=[0,2]. type=“possi” distr=“trapeze”, param vector four values. example c(0,1,2,3) corresponds possibility distribution core=[1,2] support=[0,3].
code: ninput <- 5 #Number of input parameters input <- vector(mode = "list", length = ninput) # Initialisation  input[[1]] = create_input(         name = "UER",         type = "possi",         distr = "triangle",         param = c(2.e-2, 5.7e-2, 1.e-1),         monoton = "incr"         ) input[[2]] = create_input(         name = "EF",         type = "possi",         distr = "triangle",         param = c(200, 250, 350),         monoton = "incr"         ) input[[3]] = create_input(         name = "I",         type="possi",         distr="triangle",         param=c(1,1.5,2.5),         monoton="incr"         ) input[[4]] = create_input(         name = "C",         type = "proba",         distr = "triangle",         param = c(5e-3, 20e-3, 10e-3)         ) input[[5]] = create_input(         name = "ED",         type = "proba",         distr = "triangle",         param = c(10, 50, 30)         )  ####CREATION OF THE DISTRIBUTIONS ASSOCIATED TO THE PARAMETERS input = create_distr(input)  ####VISU INPUT plot_input(input)

title: Input distribution (probability or possibility) — create_distr
code: ################################################# #### EXAMPLE 1 of Dubois & Guyonnet (2011) #### Probability and Possibility distributions ################################################# ninput = 5 #Number of input parameters input = vector(mode = "list", length = ninput) # Initialisation  input[[1]] = create_input(     name = "UER",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(2.e-2, 5.7e-2, 1.e-1),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[2]] = create_input(     name = "EF",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(200, 250, 350),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[3]] = create_input(     name = "I",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(1, 1.5, 2.5),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[4]] = create_input(     name = "C",     type = "proba",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(5e-3, 20e-3, 10e-3)     ) input[[5]] = create_input(     name = "ED",     type = "proba",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(10, 50, 30)     )  ####CREATION OF THE DISTRIBUTIONS ASSOCIATED TO THE PARAMETERS input = create_distr(input)  ####PLOT INPUTS plot_input(input)  ################################################# #### EXAMPLE 2 of Sch\"obi & Sudret (2016) #### Imprecise Probability distributions #################################################  ninput = 6 #Number of input parameters input = vector(mode = "list", length = ninput) # Initialisation  # Imprecise normal probability  # whose parameters are described by the 3rd and 5th parameters input[[1]] = create_input(     name = "A",     type = "impr proba",     distr = "normal",     param = c(3, 5),     monoton = "dunno"     )  # Imprecise normal probability # whose parameters are described by the 4th and 6th parameters input[[2]] = create_input(     name = "B",     type = "impr proba",     distr = "normal",     param = c(4, 6),     monoton = "dunno"     )  # imprecise paramters of afore-described probability distribution # mean of input number 1 as an interval input[[3]] = create_input(     name = "mu_A",     type = "possi",     distr = "interval",     param = c(-0.5, 0.5)     )  # mean of input number 2 as an interval input[[4]] = create_input(     name = "mu_B",     type = "possi",     distr = "interval",     param = c(-0.5, 0.5)     )  # standard deviation of input number 1  as an interval input[[5]] = create_input(     name = "s_A",     type = "possi",     distr = "interval",     param = c(0.7, 1)     )  # standard deviation of input number 2  as an interval input[[6]] = create_input(     name = "s_B",     type = "possi",     distr = "interval",     param = c(0.7, 1)     )  ####CREATION OF THE DISTRIBUTIONS ASSOCIATED TO THE PARAMETERS input = create_distr(input)  ####PLOT INPUTS plot_input(input)

title: Type of input variables (imprecise, random or fixed). — create_input
text: name String name input variable. type String specify type input variable: "proba": probability distribution. "possi": possibility distribution. "impr proba": imprecise probability distribution "fixed": fixed scalar value. distr String specify type distribution: type="proba" type="impr proba", distr : "normal", "lognormal","uniform", "triangle", "beta","user". type="possi", distr : "trapeze", "triangle", "interval". param Vector parameter values. depends choice distr type. type="proba" distr="normal", param vector two values. example c(0,1) corresponds normal distribution mean=0 standard deviation 1. type="proba" distr="lognormal", param vector two values. example c(0,1) corresponds lognormal distribution meanlog=0 standard deviation sdlog=1. type="proba" distr="uniform", param vector two values. example c(0,1) corresponds uniform distribution support[0,1]. type="proba" distr="triangle", param vector three values. example c(0,2,1) corresponds triangle distribution apex=1 support[0,2]. See help package triangle. type="proba" distr="beta", param vector two values. example c(1,1) corresponds beta distribution parameters shape1 shape2 equal 1 (see help qbeta stats package). type="possi" distr="trapeze", param vector four values. example c(0,1,2,3) corresponds possibility distribution core=[1,2] support=[0,3]. type="possi" distr="triangle", param vector three values. example c(0,1,2) corresponds possibility distribution core=1 support=[0,2]. type="possi" distr="interval", param vector two values. example c(0,1) corresponds interval [0,1]. type="impr proba",  		param vector integers; integer points rank input, represents uncertainty corresponding parameter chosen distribution 	        example, type="impr proba" distr="normal", c(2,3) corresponds imprecise normal distribution, whose imprecise mean input[[2]] standard deviation input[[3]]. monoton String specify monotony model function regarding input variable. "decr" decreasing. "incr" increasing. "dunno" unknown monotony known -monotony. quser distr="user", string specifies quantile function probability distribution non listed pre-defined ones. vector param updated according law. ruser distr="user", string specify random sampling function probability distribution non listed pre-defined ones. vector param updated according law.

title: Type of input variables (imprecise, random or fixed). — create_input
code: ################################################# #### EXAMPLE 1 of Dubois & Guyonnet (2011) #### Probability and Possibility distributions #################################################  ninput <- 5 #Number of input parameters input <- vector(mode="list", length=ninput) # Initialisation  input[[1]] = create_input(     name="UER",     type="possi",     distr="triangle",     param=c(2.e-2, 5.7e-2, 1.e-1),     monoton="incr"     ) input[[2]] = create_input(     name="EF",     type="possi",     distr="triangle",     param=c(200, 250, 350),     monoton="incr"     ) input[[3]] = create_input(     name="I",     type="possi",     distr="triangle",     param=c(1, 1.5, 2.5),     monoton="incr"     ) input[[4]] = create_input(     name="C",     type="proba",     distr="triangle",     param=c(5e-3, 20e-3, 10e-3)     ) input[[5]] = create_input(     name="ED",     type="proba",     distr="triangle",     param=c(10, 50, 30)     )      ################################################# #### EXAMPLE 2 of Sch\"obi & Sudret (2016) #### Imprecise Probability distributions #################################################  ninput <- 6 #Number of input parameters input<-vector(mode="list", length=ninput) # Initialisation  # Imprecise normal probability  # whose parameters are described by the 3rd and 5th parameters input[[1]] = create_input(     name="A",     type="impr proba",     distr="normal",     param=c(3, 5),     monoton="dunno"     )  # Imprecise normal probability # whose parameters are described by the 4th and 6th parameters input[[2]] = create_input(     name="B",     type="impr proba",     distr="normal",     param=c(4, 6),     monoton="dunno"     )  # imprecise paramters of afore-described probability distribution # mean of input number 1 as an interval input[[3]] = create_input(     name="mu_A",     type="possi",     distr="interval",     param=c(-0.5, 0.5)     )  # mean of input number 2 as an interval input[[4]] = create_input(     name="mu_B",     type="possi",     distr="interval",     param=c(-0.5, 0.5)     )  # standard deviation of input number 1  as an interval input[[5]] = create_input(     name="s_A",     type="possi",     distr="interval",     param=c(0.7, 1)     )  # standard deviation of input number 2  as an interval input[[6]] = create_input(     name="s_B",     type="possi",     distr="interval",     param=c(0.7, 1)     )

title: Create a user-defined possibility — create_possi_user
text: listX  type="confidence", listX corresponds list endpoints considered intervals. instance, c(0, 1, 2, 3) corresponds intervals [0,1], [1,2] [2,3]. type="bracket", listX corresponds mean value.  ListL  type="confidence", listL corresponds list possibility degrees. instance, c(0.2, 1, 0.05) corresponds 3 intervals, degrees 0.2, 1.0 0.05. type="bracket", listL corresponds vector two values: standard deviation limit truncation (expressed number standard deviation). instance, c(0.1,3) corresponds standard deviation 0.1 limit 3 times standard deviation.

title: Create a user-defined possibility — create_possi_user
text: Details approach using confidence interval, see Baudrit et al. 2006. Details approach using inequality Bienayme-Chebychev, see Sect. 3.3 https://www.irit.fr/~Henri.Prade/Papers/DFoulMauP.pdf

title: Create a user-defined possibility — create_possi_user
code: ################################################# ### user-defined ### mode: "confidence" ### user information: series of intervals with associated degree of possibilites ################################################# listX = c(0.3, 0.52, 0.98, 1.5, 2, 2.5)## liste des bornes des intervalles de confiance listL = c(0.33, 1, 0.2, 0.05, 0.05) ## liste des niveaux de possibilité (nbre = nbre_listX-1) a = create_possi_user(listX, listL, type = "confidence") plot(a)   ################################################# ### mode: "bracket" ### inegalite de Bienayme-Chebychev; voir: sect. 3.3 de https://www.irit.fr/~Henri.Prade/Papers/DFoulMauP.pdf ### user information: mean, standard deviation and limit of truncation ################################################# listX = c(0.5) ## mean listL = c(0.1, 3) ## standard dev. + limit of truncation (in number of standard dev.) b = create_possi_user(listX, listL, type = "bracket") plot(b)

what: Hybrid Methods For Addressing Uncertainty In RISK Assessments — hyriskR-package
title: Hybrid Methods For Addressing Uncertainty In RISK Assessments — hyriskR-package
text: Methods addressing uncertainty risk assessments using hybrid representations uncertainty (probability distributions, fuzzy numbers, intervals, probability distributions imprecise parameters). uncertainty propagation procedure combines random sampling using Monte Carlo method fuzzy interval analysis Baudrit et al. (2007) <doi:10.1109/TFUZZ.2006.876720>. sensitivity analysis based pinching method Ferson Tucker (2006) <doi:10.1016/j.ress.2005.11.052>.

title: Hybrid Methods For Addressing Uncertainty In RISK Assessments — hyriskR-package
text: package provides tools uncertainty analysis: Create input uncertain variables represented interval, possibility distribution (trapezoidal triangular), probability distribution (normal, lognormal, beta, triangle, Gumbel user-defined), imprecise probability distribution. Perform joint uncertainty propagation using IRS Baudrit et al. (2007) Hybrid algorithm described Baudrit et al. (2006). Perform uncertainty propagation random variables represented imprecise probabilities, .e. probability distribution ill-known parameters (Pedroni et al., 2013). Improve speed uncertainty propagation via low discrepancy random sequences surrogate-based methods (Lockwood et al., 2012). Summarize uncertan results form pair lower upper cumulative distribution functions CDFs. Summarize uncertainty form interval exceedance probabilities, interval quantiles, global indicator corresponding area lower upper CDFs. Perform sensitivity analysis using pinching approach (Ferson Tucker, 2006) regional approach (Rohmer Verdel, 2014).

title: Joint propagation of uncertainty — propag
text: N Integer corresponding number random samples. Depending type sampler used, number shoudl order several hundreds. input List inputs provided function CREATE_INPUT(). FUN Model assessment function. choice_opt Option constrainted optimization algorithm: "L-BFGS-B": Limited-memory BFGS based optimr package. "L-BFGS-B_MULTI": Limited-memory BFGS multiple starts. "GENOUD": genetic algorithm plus derivative optimizer based rgenoud package, see Mebane & Sekhon (2011). param_opt Parameters needed optimization algorithm: "L-BFGS-B": param_opt=NULL. "L-BFGS-B_MULTI": param_opt specifies number multi starts (e.g. 10). "GENOUD": param_opt specifies vector paramters (population size, maxixmum generations, solution tolerance), see Mebane & Sekhon (2011). mode Type hybrid uncertainty propagation: "IRS" Independent Random Sampling Baudrit et al. (2007). "HYBRID" Hybrid propagation described Baudrit et al. (2006). corr Tolerance avoid empty alpha-cuts. default, corr=0.01. NL Integer specify number alpha-cuts needed hybrid propagation described Baudrit et al. (2006). default, NL=10. sampler Type random sampler used. Three options available either Latin Hypercube Design (lhs) developed MacKay et al. (1979), Space-Filling Designs based Strauss process (strauss) investigated Franco et al. (2008), original sampler R (base).  default, sampler="lhs". return.r Logical return random values generated sample inputs. advanced users.

title: Joint propagation of uncertainty — propag
code: if (FALSE) {  ################################################# #### EXAMPLE 1 of Dubois & Guyonnet (2011) #### Probability and Possibility distributions #################################################  #### Model function FUN = function(X){   UER = X[1]   EF = X[2]   I = X[3]   C = X[4]   ED = X[5]   return(UER*I*C*EF*ED/(70*70*365)) }  ninput = 5 #Number of input parameters input = vector(mode = "list", length = ninput) # Initialisation  input[[1]] = create_input(     name = "UER",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(2.e-2, 5.7e-2, 1.e-1),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[2]] = create_input(     name = "EF",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(200, 250, 350),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[3]] = create_input(     name = "I",     type="possi",     distr="triangle",     param=c(1,1.5,2.5),     monoton="incr"     ) input[[4]] = create_input(     name = "C",     type = "proba",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(5e-3, 20e-3, 10e-3)     ) input[[5]] = create_input(     name = "ED",     type = "proba",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(10, 50, 30)     )  ####CREATION OF THE DISTRIBUTIONS ASSOCIATED TO THE PARAMETERS input = create_distr(input)  ####VISU INPUT plot_input(input)  ################################################# #### PROPAGATION  #OPTIMZATION CHOICES choice_opt = NULL #no optimization needed param_opt = NULL  #PROPAGATION RUN Z0_IRS = propag(N = 1000, input, FUN, choice_opt, param_opt, mode = "IRS") Z0_HYBRID = propag(N = 250,input, FUN, choice_opt, param_opt, mode = "HYBRID")  ################################################# #### POST-PROCESSING  # VISU - PROPAGATION plot_cdf(Z0_IRS, xlab = "Z", ylab = "CDF", main = "EX 1", lwd = 1.5) plot_cdf(Z0_HYBRID, new = FALSE, color1 = 3, color2 = 4, lwd = 1.5)  ################################################# #### EXAMPLE 2 of Schobi & Sudret (2016) #### Imprecise Probability distributions #################################################  #### Model function FUN = function(X){   A = X[1]   B = X[2]   return(100*(B-A^2)^2+(1-A)^2) }  ninput<-6 #Number of input parameters input<-vector(mode="list", length=ninput) # Initialisation  # Imprecise normal probability  # whose parameters are described by the 3rd and 5th parameters input[[1]] = create_input(     name = "A",     type = "impr proba",     distr = "normal",     param = c(3, 5),     monoton = "dunno"     )  # Imprecise normal probability # whose parameters are described by the 4th and 6th parameters input[[2]] = create_input(     name = "B",     type = "impr proba",     distr = "normal",     param = c(4, 6),     monoton = "dunno"     )   # imprecise paramters of afore-described probability distribution # mean of input number 1 as an interval input[[3]] = create_input(     name = "mu_A",     type = "possi",     distr = "interval",     param = c(-0.5, 0.5)     )  # mean of input number 2 as an interval input[[4]] = create_input(     name = "mu_B",     type = "possi",     distr = "interval",     param = c(-0.5, 0.5)     )  # standard deviation of input number 1  as an interval input[[5]] = create_input(     name = "s_A",     type = "possi",     distr = "interval",     param = c(0.7, 1)     )  # standard deviation of input number 2  as an interval input[[6]] = create_input(     name = "s_B",     type = "possi",     distr = "interval",     param = c(0.7, 1)     )   ####CREATION OF THE DISTRIBUTIONS ASSOCIATED TO THE PARAMETERS input = create_distr(input)  ####VISU INPUT (needs propagation parameters to plot impr proba distributions) plot_input(input)  ################################################# ### PROPAGATION # OPTIMZATION CHOICES (could take some time) choice_opt = "GENOUD" param_opt = c(50, 3, 1.e-1)  #PROPAGATION RUN Z0_IRS = propag(N = 1000, input, FUN, choice_opt, param_opt, mode = "IRS")  ################################################# ### VISU - PROPAGATION plot_cdf(Z0_IRS, xlab = "Z", ylab = "CDF", main = "EX 2", lwd = 1.5)  }

code: if (FALSE) { ################################################# #### EXAMPLE 1 of Dubois & Guyonnet (2011) #### Probability and Possibility distributions #################################################  #### Model function FUN = function(X){   UER = X[1]   EF = X[2]   I = X[3]   C = X[4]   ED = X[5]   return(UER*I*C*EF*ED/(70*70*365)) }  ninput = 5 #Number of input parameters input = vector(mode="list", length=ninput) # Initialisation  input[[1]] = create_input(     name = "UER",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(2.e-2, 5.7e-2, 1.e-1),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[2]] = create_input(     name = "EF",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(200,250,350),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[3]] = create_input(     name = "I",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(1,1.5,2.5),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[4]] = create_input(     name = "C",     type = "proba",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(5e-3,20e-3,10e-3)     ) input[[5]] = create_input(     name = "ED",     type = "proba",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(10,50,30)     )  ####CREATION OF THE DISTRIBUTIONS ASSOCIATED TO THE PARAMETERS input = create_distr(input)  ####VISU INPUT plot_input(input)  ################################################# #### PROPAGATION  #OPTIMZATION CHOICES choice_opt=NULL #no optimization needed param_opt=NULL  #PROPAGATION RUN Z0_IRS = propag(N = 250, input, FUN, choice_opt, param_opt, mode = "IRS")  ################################################# #### PINCHING Z0p = pinching_fun(   which=1,##first input variable   value = 5.7e-2, ##pinched at the scalar value of 5.7e-2   N = 250,   input,   FUN,   choice_opt,   param_opt,   mode="IRS"   )  # VISU - PROPAGATION plot_cdf(Z0_IRS, xlab = "Z", ylab = "CDF", main = "EX PINCHING", lwd=1.5) plot_cdf(Z0p, color1 = 3, color2 = 4, new = FALSE, lwd = 1.5)  # sensitivity measure sensi.global = sensi_pinching(Z0_IRS, Z0p) print(paste("global sensitivity measure: ",sensi.global,sep=""))  }

code: if (FALSE) { ################################################# #### EXAMPLE 1 of Dubois & Guyonnet (2011) #### Probability and Possibility distributions #################################################  #### Model function FUN = function(X){   UER = X[1]   EF = X[2]   I = X[3]   C = X[4]   ED = X[5]   return(UER*I*C*EF*ED/(70*70*365)) }  ninput = 5 #Number of input parameters input = vector(mode = "list", length = ninput) # Initialisation  input[[1]] = create_input(     name = "UER",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(2.e-2, 5.7e-2, 1.e-1),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[2]] = create_input(     name = "EF",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(200, 250, 350),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[3]] = create_input(     name = "I",     type = "possi",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(1, 1.5, 2.5),     monoton = "incr"     ) input[[4]] = create_input(     name = "C",     type = "proba",     distr = "triangle",     param=c(5e-3, 20e-3, 10e-3)     ) input[[5]] = create_input(     name = "ED",     type = "proba",     distr = "triangle",     param = c(10, 50, 30)     )  ####CREATION OF THE DISTRIBUTIONS ASSOCIATED TO THE PARAMETERS input = create_distr(input)  ####VISU INPUT plot_input(input)  ################################################# #### PROPAGATION  #OPTIMZATION CHOICES choice_opt = NULL #no optimization needed param_opt = NULL  #PROPAGATION RUN Z0_IRS = propag(N = 1000, input, FUN, choice_opt, param_opt, mode = "IRS")  ################################################# #### POST-PROCESSING  # VISU - PROPAGATION plot_cdf(Z0_IRS, xlab = "Z", ylab = "CDF", main = "EX 1", lwd = 1.5)  # interval of quantiles level = 0.95 quant = quan_interval(Z0_IRS, level) Qlw = quant$Qlow Qup = uant$Qupp print(paste("interval of quantiles at level:",level," : ",   "Qlow:",round(Qlw/10^floor(log10(Qlw)),   abs(floor(log10((Qup-Qlw)/10^ceiling(log10(Qlw))))))*10^floor(log10(Qlw)),   " & Qup:",round(Qup/10^floor(log10(Qup)),   abs(floor(log10((Qup-Qlw)/10^ceiling(log10(Qup))))))*10^floor(log10(Qup)),sep="")   )  # interval of probabilities thres = 1e-5 prob = proba_interval(Z0_IRS, thres) print(paste("interval of probabilities at threshold:",thres," : ",   "Plow:",prob$Plow,   " & Pup:",round(prob$Pupp,3),sep="")   )  # Global indicator of uncertainty unc = uncertainty(Z0_IRS) print(paste("Epistemic uncertainty : ",unc,sep=""))  }
